A. Direct mode
B. Indirect mode
C. Relative mode
D. Indexed mode

sourceQuestion:
Match the pairs in the following questions:

(A) Base addressing        (p) Reentrant
(B) Indexed addressing     (q) Accumulator
(C) Stack addressing       (r) Array
(D) Implied addressing     (s) Position independent

A. A-p, B-r, C-q, D-s
B. A-s, B-r, C-p, D-q
C. A-q, B-r, C-p, D-s
D. A-r, B-p, C-q, D-s

options.text: Implied addressing (s) Position independent A. A-p, B-r, C-q, D-s B. A-s, B-r, C-p, D-q C. A-q, B-r, C-p, D-s D. A-r, B-p, C-q, D-s

A. Co-routines
B. Position-independent code
C. Shareable code
D. Interrupt Handlers

sourceQuestion:

A. Indirect addressing
B. Indexed addressing
C. Base register addressing
D. PC relative addressing

sourceQuestion:

A. Pointers
B. Arrays
C. Records
D. Recursive procedures with local variables

sourceQuestion:
The most appropriate matching for the following pairs is:

X: Indirect addressing    1: Loops
Y: Immediate addressing   2: Pointers
Z: Auto decrement addressing  3: Constants

A. X - 1, Y - 3, Z - 2
B. X - 3, Y - 2, Z - 1
C. X - 2, Y - 3, Z - 1
D. X - 3, Y - 1, Z - 2

sourceQuestion:
Which is the most appropriate match for the items in the first column with the items in the second column?

First Column:
X. Indirect Addressing
Y. Indexed Addressing
Z. Base Register Addressing

Second Column:
I.  Array implementation
II. Writing relocatable code
III. Passing array as parameter

A. (X, III), (Y, I), (Z, II)
B. (X, II), (Y, III), (Z, I)
C. (X, III), (Y, II), (Z, I)
D. (X, I), (Y, III), (Z, II)

I.  Array implementation
II. Writing relocatable code
III. Passing array as parameter

sourceQuestion:
In the absolute addressing mode:
A. the operand is inside the instruction
B. the address of the operand is inside the instruction
C. the register containing the address of the operand is specified inside the instruction
D. the location of the operand is implicit
sourceAnswer: B. the address of the operand is inside the instruction

parsed.stem: In the absolute addressing mode:

options.text: the operand is inside the instruction
the address of the operand is inside the instruction
the register containing the address of the operand is specified inside the instruction
the location of the operand is implicit

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following addressing modes are suitable for program relocation at run time?
I.   Absolute addressing
II.  Based addressing
III. Relative addressing
IV.  Indirect addressing

A. I and IV
B. I and II
C. II and III
D. I, II and IV

parsed.stem:
Which of the following addressing modes are suitable for program relocation at run time?
I.   Absolute addressing
II.  Based addressing
III. Relative addressing
IV.  Indirect addressing

sourceQuestion:
Consider a three-word machine instruction:

  ADD  A[R_i],  @B

The first operand (destination) uses indexed addressing mode with R_i as the index register. The second operand (source) uses indirect addressing mode. A and B are memory addresses residing at the second and third words of the instruction, respectively. The first word of the instruction specifies the opcode and the two addressing modes.

During the execution cycle of this instruction, the number of memory references required (excluding the instruction fetch) is:

A. 3
B. 4
C. 5
D. 6

parsed.stem:
Consider a three-word machine instruction:

  ADD  A[R_i],  @B

The first operand (destination) uses indexed addressing mode with R_i as the index register. The second operand (source) uses indirect addressing mode. A and B are memory addresses residing at the second and third words of the instruction, respectively. The first word of the instruction specifies the opcode and the two addressing modes.

During the execution cycle of this instruction, the number of memory references required (excluding the instruction fetch) is:

sourceQuestion:
Match each of the high level language statements given on the left hand side with the most natural addressing mode from those listed on the right hand side.

Left Column:
(1) A[J] = B[J]
(2) while (*A++)
(3) int temp = *z

Right Column:
(a) Indirect addressing
(b) Indexed addressing
(c) Auto increment

A. (1, c), (2, b), (3, a)
B. (1, c), (2, a), (3, b)
C. (1, b), (2, c), (3, a)
D. (1, b), (2, a), (3, c)

parsed.stem:
Match each of the high level language statements given on the left hand side with the most natural addressing mode from those listed on the right hand side.

Left Column:
(1) A[J] = B[J]
(2) while (*A++)
(3) int temp = *z

Right Column:
(a) Indirect addressing
(b) Indexed addressing
(c) Auto increment

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is/are true of the auto-increment addressing mode?
I. It is useful in creating self-relocating code
II. If it is included in an Instruction Set Architecture, then an additional ALU is required for effective address calculation
III. The amount of increment depends on the size of the data item accessed

A. I only
B. II only
C. III only
D. II and III only

parsed.stem:
Which of the following is/are true of the auto-increment addressing mode?
I. It is useful in creating self-relocating code
II. If it is included in an Instruction Set Architecture, then an additional ALU is required for effective address calculation
III. The amount of increment depends on the size of the data item accessed

sourceQuestion:
Consider a hypothetical processor with an instruction of type LW R1, 20(R2), which during execution reads a 32-bit word from memory and stores it in register R1. The effective address of the memory location is obtained by the addition of a constant 20 and the contents of register R2. Which of the following best reflects the addressing mode implemented by this instruction for the operand in memory?

A. Immediate addressing
B. Register addressing
C. Scaled addressing
D. Base Indexed Addressing

parsed.stem: Consider a hypothetical processor with an instruction of type LW R1, 20(R2), which during execution reads a 32-bit word from memory and stores it in register R1. The effective address of the memory location is obtained by the addition of a constant 20 and the contents of register R2. Which of the following best reflects the addressing mode implemented by this instruction for the operand in memory?

sourceAnswer: D. Index addressing mode, with X as an offset represented in 2's complement 16-bit representation

options.text: Post-increment addressing mode, with (R1) as the effective address
Pre-decrement addressing mode, with (R1) as the effective address
Register direct addressing mode, with R1 as the effective address
Index addressing mode, with X as an offset represented in 2's complement 16-bit representation

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements about relative addressing mode is FALSE?

A. It enables reduced instruction size
B. It allows indexing of array element with same instruction
C. It enables easy relocation of data
D. It allows faster effective address calculation than absolute addressing
sourceAnswer: B. It allows indexing of array element with same instruction

parsed.stem: Which of the following statements about relative addressing mode is FALSE?

options.text: It allows indexing of array element with same instruction
It enables easy relocation of data
It allows faster effective address calculation than absolute addressing

sourceQuestion:
The memory locations 1000 and 1010 have data values 18 and 16 respectively before the following program is executed.

MOVI  R_0, 1          ; Move immediate
LOAD  R_0, 1000(R_0)  ; Load from memory
ADDI  R_0, 1000       ; Add immediate
STOREI 0(R_0), 20     ; Store immediate

Which of the statements below is TRUE after the program is executed?

A. Memory location 1000 has value 20
B. Memory location 1020 has value 20
C. Memory location 1002 has value 20
D. Memory location 1001 has value 20

parsed.stem:
The memory locations 1000 and 1010 have data values 18 and 16 respectively before the following program is executed.

MOVI  R_0, 1          ; Move immediate
LOAD  R_0, 1000(R_0)  ; Load from memory
ADDI  R_0, 1000       ; Add immediate
STOREI 0(R_0), 20     ; Store immediate

Which of the statements below is TRUE after the program is executed?

sourceQuestion:
The main difference(s) between a CISC and a RISC processor is/are that a RISC processor typically

A. has fewer instructions
B. has fewer addressing modes
C. has more registers
D. is easier to implement using hardwired logic

parsed.stem: The main difference(s) between a CISC and a RISC processor is/are that a RISC processor typically

options.text: is easier to implement using hardwired logic

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following processor design characteristics:
I. Register-to-register arithmetic operations only
II. Fixed-length instruction format
III. Hardwired control unit

Which of the characteristics above are used in the design of a RISC processor?

A. I and II only
B. II and III only
C. I and III only
D. I, II and III

parsed.stem:
Consider the following processor design characteristics:
I. Register-to-register arithmetic operations only
II. Fixed-length instruction format
III. Hardwired control unit

Which of the characteristics above are used in the design of a RISC processor?

sourceQuestion:
In the three-level memory hierarchy shown in the following table, p_i denotes the probability that an access request will refer to M_i.

Hierarchy Level | Access Time (t_i) | Probability of Access (p_i) | Page Transfer Time (T_i)
M_1 (Cache memory) | 10^-8 sec | 0.99000 | --
M_2 (Main memory) | 10^-5 sec | 0.00998 | 0.001 sec
M_3 (Disk memory) | 10^-2 sec | 0.00002 | 0.1 sec

If a miss occurs at level M_i, a page is transferred from M_{i+1} to M_i and the average time required for a processor to read one word from this memory system is t_A. Calculate the average time t_A required for a processor to read one word from this memory system.

A. 1.5 * 10^-8  B. 2.5 * 10^-8  C. 3 * 10^-5  D. None of these

parsed.stem:
In the three-level memory hierarchy shown in the following table, p_i denotes the probability that an access request will refer to M_i.

Hierarchy Level | Access Time (t_i) | Probability of Access (p_i) | Page Transfer Time (T_i)
M_1 (Cache memory) | 10^-8 sec | 0.99000 | --
M_2 (Main memory) | 10^-5 sec | 0.00998 | 0.001 sec
M_3 (Disk memory) | 10^-2 sec | 0.00002 | 0.1 sec

If a miss occurs at level M_i, a page is transferred from M_{i+1} to M_i and the average time required for a processor to read one word from this memory system is t_A. Calculate the average time t_A required for a processor to read one word from this memory system.

A. Interrupts
B. DMA
C. Polling
D. Cache Memory

sourceQuestion:
A computer system has a 4K word cache organized in block-set-associative manner with 4 blocks per set, 64 words per block. The number of bits in the SET and WORD fields of the main memory address format is:
A. 15, 40
B. 6, 4
C. 7, 2
D. 4, 6

parsed.stem: A computer system has a 4K word cache organized in block-set-associative manner with 4 blocks per set, 64 words per block. The number of bits in the SET and WORD fields of the main memory address format is:

sourceQuestion:
The main memory of a computer has 2^n blocks while the cache has 2^k blocks. If the cache uses the set associative mapping scheme with 2^r blocks per set, then block j of the main memory maps to the set:
A. (j mod 2^n) of the cache
B. (j mod 2^k) of the cache
C. (j mod 2^(k-r)) of the cache
D. (j mod 2^r) of the cache

parsed.stem: The main memory of a computer has 2^n blocks while the cache has 2^k blocks. If the cache uses the set associative mapping scheme with 2^r blocks per set, then block j of the main memory maps to the set:

sourceQuestion:
More than one word are put in one cache block to:
A. exploit the temporal locality of reference in a program
B. exploit the spatial locality of reference in a program
C. reduce the miss penalty
D. none of the above
sourceAnswer: B. exploit the spatial locality of reference in a program

parsed.stem: More than one word are put in one cache block to:

options.text: exploit the temporal locality of reference in a program
exploit the spatial locality of reference in a program

sourceQuestion:
Consider a small two-way set-associative cache memory, consisting of four blocks. For choosing the block to be replaced, use the least recently used (LRU) scheme. The number of cache misses for the following sequence of block addresses is:
8, 12, 0, 12, 8

A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 5

parsed.stem:
Consider a small two-way set-associative cache memory, consisting of four blocks. For choosing the block to be replaced, use the least recently used (LRU) scheme. The number of cache misses for the following sequence of block addresses is:
8, 12, 0, 12, 8

A. 10, 17
B. 10, 22
C. 15, 17
D. 5, 17

sourceQuestion:
Consider two cache organizations. First one is a 32 KB 8-way set associative with 32-byte block size, the second is of the same size but direct mapped. The size of an address is 32 bits in both cases. A 2-to-1 multiplexer has latency of 2 ns while a 1-bit comparator has latency of 1 ns. The hit latency of the set associative organization is h_1, while that of the direct mapped organization is h_2.

The value of h_1 is:

A. 2.5 ns
B. 2.3 ns
C. 1.9 ns
D. 1.7 ns
